title: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY
items.title: Cardiovascular Diseases: Prevalence, Gender Differences, and Risk Factors
items.content: Cardiovascular diseases remain a leading cause of death globally, affecting nearly half of the adult population. Despite declines in age-adjusted mortality rates from coronary heart disease due to risk factor management and improved treatments, cardiovascular diseases still account for about 700,000 deaths annually in the U.S., with one-third being sudden. The prevalence is increasing in developing nations and women are now experiencing a rise in cardiovascular-related deaths compared to men. Factors like inflammation, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome play significant roles, particularly in women.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular diseases cause nearly 700,000 deaths annually in the U.S., with one-third sudden.
Age-adjusted death rates for coronary heart disease have declined by two-thirds over four decades.
Globally, cardiovascular diseases result in approximately 19 million deaths.
The prevalence of obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome threatens progress in reducing mortality from coronary heart disease.
Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death for both women and men.
Deaths due to cardiovascular disease have declined in men but increased in women over recent decades.
Coronary artery disease in women often involves microcirculation dysfunction, with lower diagnostic accuracy using exercise electrocardiography compared to men.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY
items.title: Natural History and Early Detection in Cardiovascular Disorders
items.content: Cardiovascular disorders can present acutely, such as an acute myocardial infarction or sudden death due to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. However, early recognition of risk factors can prevent these events. For instance, recognizing and managing atherosclerosis risk factors could delay or prevent myocardial infarctions. Similarly, identifying heart murmurs and family history in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy allows for early diagnosis and treatment. Conversely, valvular heart disease and idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy may progress with chronic symptoms before acute deterioration.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular disorders can present acutely or be detected early through risk factor recognition.
Early management of atherosclerosis risk factors might prevent myocardial infarctions.
Recognition of heart murmurs and family history in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy allows for early diagnosis.
Valvular heart disease and idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy often have a prolonged course with chronic symptoms before acute episodes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: CARDIAC SYMPTOMS
items.title: Understanding the Exertional Nature of Cardiac Symptoms
items.content: Cardiac symptoms like dyspnea and chest discomfort are typically exertion-related in heart disease, contrasting with their rare occurrence at rest. This pattern helps differentiate cardiac causes from other conditions.
keyPoints: Dyspnea and/or chest discomfort during activity suggest heart disease.
Symptoms appearing at rest and remitting with exertion are rarely due to heart disease.
Careful questioning about symptom relation to exertion is crucial.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · CARDIAC SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: CARDIAC SYMPTOMS
items.title: Identifying High-Risk Asymptomatic Patients for CAD
items.content: Asymptomatic individuals may still be at risk for catastrophic cardiac events. Assessing global CAD risk involves clinical assessment, cholesterol measurement, and biomarkers like C-reactive protein.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic patients can present with abnormal findings such as heart murmurs or ECG abnormalities.
Global CAD risk should be assessed using clinical evaluation and biomarkers.
C-reactive protein may be used in some patients for risk assessment.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · CARDIAC SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Importance of Comprehensive Cardiac Diagnosis
items.content: A complete cardiac diagnosis is crucial for formulating an effective therapeutic strategy and prognosis. It involves identifying the underlying etiology, anatomical abnormalities, functional disability, and physiologic disturbances.
keyPoints: Etiology: congenital, hypertensive, ischemic, inflammatory
Anatomical abnormalities: chamber involvement (hypertrophied, dilated), valve issues (regurgitant, stenotic), pericardial involvement, myocardial infarction
Functional disability: NYHA classification for symptom elicitation
Physiologic disturbances: arrhythmia, congestive heart failure, myocardial ischemia

Therapeutic strategy depends on identifying underlying anatomic abnormalities (e.g., coronary atherosclerosis, aortic stenosis) and contributory physiologic disturbances (e.g., severe anemia, thyrotoxicosis, supraventricular tachycardia)
Severity of disability influences workup extent and therapeutic strategy
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Role of Clinical Examination in Cardiac Diagnosis
items.content: The clinical examination is foundational for diagnosing cardiac disorders, supplemented by various laboratory tests to confirm findings.
keyPoints: Clinical examination: basis for diagnosis of many cardiac disorders
Supplementary tests: ECG, noninvasive imaging (chest x-ray, echocardiogram, radionuclide imaging, CT, PET, MRI), blood tests (lipid determinations, C-reactive protein, BNP)
Specialized invasive exams: cardiac catheterization, coronary arteriography
Genetic tests: identify monogenic diseases (hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Marfan's syndrome, QT interval prolongation)
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY | ASSESSMENT OF FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT
items.title: Importance of Family History in Cardiovascular Risk Assessment
items.content: Family history is crucial for assessing cardiovascular disease risk due to the common familial clustering of heart diseases. Mendelian transmission can be seen in conditions like hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Marfan's syndrome, and prolonged QT syndrome, while polygenic disorders such as premature coronary disease, essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia also exhibit familial patterns.
keyPoints: Familial clustering is common in many forms of heart disease.
Mendelian transmission occurs in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Marfan's syndrome, and prolonged QT syndrome.
Premature coronary disease, essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia are usually polygenic disorders.
Familial patterns may also be influenced by dietary or behavior patterns like excessive salt or calorie intake and smoking.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · FAMILY HISTORY | ASSESSMENT OF FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT

items.subtopic: FAMILY HISTORY | ASSESSMENT OF FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT
items.title: Assessing Functional Impairment in Heart Disease Patients
items.content: Functional impairment assessment involves evaluating the level of activity a patient can perform before symptoms develop. It is important to consider not just the presence of dyspnea but also its onset relative to physical exertion, such as breathlessness after running stairs versus walking on flat ground. The history should include changes in specific tasks over time and the impact of therapeutic regimens.
keyPoints: Assess activity level before symptom onset for accurate functional impairment evaluation.
Breathlessness after minimal exertion (e.g., a few steps) indicates more severe impairment than breathlessness after significant exertion (e.g., two flights of stairs).
Consider the patient's usual physical activity and any changes in capability over time, such as tasks they could perform 6 months or 1 year ago.
Evaluate the impact of treatment; persistence of symptoms despite optimal therapy indicates more severe disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · FAMILY HISTORY | ASSESSMENT OF FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH A HEART MURMUR
items.title: Interpreting ECG in the Context of Cardiovascular Findings
items.content: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is essential for patients with known or suspected heart disease, but it should be interpreted alongside other cardiovascular findings due to its limitations. While useful for identifying arrhythmias, conduction abnormalities, ventricular hypertrophy, and acute myocardial infarction, ECG changes can be influenced by noncardiac factors such as age, body habitus, and serum electrolyte concentrations.
keyPoints: ECG is crucial for diagnosing arrhythmias, conduction abnormalities, ventricular hypertrophy, and acute myocardial infarction.
Interpret ECG changes in the context of other cardiovascular findings due to wide normal range and noncardiac influences.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH A HEART MURMUR

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH A HEART MURMUR
items.title: Evaluating Heart Murmurs in Asymptomatic Individuals
items.content: Most heart murmurs are midsystolic and soft (grades I–II/VI) and often benign, especially in asymptomatic children or young adults without other signs of heart disease. In such cases, echocardiography is generally not required.
keyPoints: Murmurs that are midsystolic and grades I–II/VI are typically benign in asymptomatic individuals.
Echocardiography is usually unnecessary for these patients without additional evidence of heart disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH A HEART MURMUR

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY
items.question: What has contributed to the decline in age-adjusted death rates for coronary heart disease over the past four decades in the United States?
options: Identification and reduction of risk factors, improved treatments, and interventions
Increased prevalence of obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus
Higher incidence of sudden deaths due to cardiovascular diseases
Greater diagnostic accuracy of exercise electrocardiography in women
items.explanation: The decline is attributed to the identification and reduction of risk factors, as well as improved treatments and interventions.

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY
items.question: Which factor is more prominently associated with coronary atherosclerosis in women compared to men?
options: Coronary artery disease (CAD) due to epicardial obstruction
Inflammation, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome
Higher age-adjusted death rates from cardiovascular diseases
Reduced diagnostic accuracy of exercise electrocardiography in men
items.explanation: Inflammation, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and the metabolic syndrome are more prominent risk factors for coronary atherosclerosis in women.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY
items.question: What is a common acute presentation of cardiovascular disorders?
options: Gradual increase in dyspnea over several years
Development of an acute myocardial infarction in a previously asymptomatic person
Stable heart murmur without any other symptoms
Prolonged QT interval with no clinical manifestations
items.explanation: Cardiovascular disorders often present acutely, such as an acute myocardial infarction in a previously asymptomatic person.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease | THE MAGNITUDE OF THE PROBLEM | NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: CARDIAC SYMPTOMS
items.question: Which symptom most frequently manifests as a result of myocardial ischemia?
items.explanation: Ischemia, caused by an imbalance between the heart's oxygen supply and demand, is most frequently manifested as chest discomfort.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · CARDIAC SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: CARDIAC SYMPTOMS
items.question: What symptom pattern during exertion is characteristic of patients with heart disease?
options: Symptoms appearing at rest and remitting during exertion
Symptoms appearing during activity and remitting at rest
No symptoms either at rest or during exertion
items.explanation: Dyspnea and/or chest discomfort that appear during activity are characteristic of patients with heart disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · CARDIAC SYMPTOMS

items.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is an example of a Mendelian transmission of single-gene defects.

items.question: What are the most important risk factors for coronary artery disease (CAD) mentioned in the passage?
options: Premature coronary disease, essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Marfan's syndrome, and prolonged QT syndrome
Excessive ingestion of salt or calories and cigarette smoking
items.explanation: Premature coronary disease, essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia are the most important risk factors for CAD.

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT
items.question: What does breathlessness after taking a few steps on level ground indicate about functional impairment?
options: Less significant than breathlessness after running up two flights of stairs
More significant than breathlessness after running up two flights of stairs
Equivalent to breathlessness after running up two flights of stairs
items.explanation: Breathlessness after taking a few steps on level ground denotes more significant functional impairment than symptoms occurring after running up two flights of stairs.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Possible Cardiovascular Disease · FAMILY HISTORY | ASSESSMENT OF FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT

items.question: Which of the following conditions can an ECG specifically diagnose?
items.explanation: An ECG can specifically diagnose arrhythmias, conduction abnormalities, ventricular hypertrophy, and acute myocardial infarction.

In all patients with any type of heart murmur

In symptomatic adults with loud systolic murmurs
